trust_assumption: A DeFi protocol that switched from spot price to a time-weighted average price (TWAP) oracle trusts that averaging over a window of recent observations makes the reported price economically infeasible to manipulate, so it treats the TWAP read at the moment of a liquidation, mint, or collateral revaluation as manipulation-resistant ground truth and sizes its safety margins accordingly.
violation: An attacker who controls block production cadence (a proposer/builder via MEV markets, an L2 sequencer, or a low-liquidity pool on a chain with long or irregular block times) skews the observations that fall inside the TWAP window — stuffing or spacing blocks so a brief manipulated price dominates the time-weighting — and extracts value during the exact transaction that reads the average, defeating the window's averaging by warping the time dimension the average is computed over rather than the prices themselves.

techniques.name: Block-cadence warping of TWAP observation window
Sequencer-controlled timestamp skew of weighted price

rationale: After the flash-loan spot-oracle disasters, the entire DeFi industry treated TWAP as the cure, and 2025-2027 protocols on app-chains, rollups, and alt-L1s deploy short-window TWAPs as their canonical manipulation-resistant feed. The overlooked seam is that TWAP's security rests on an assumption about *time* — that observations are sampled at honest, roughly-uniform intervals — yet on chains where the attacker influences block timing (centralized L2 sequencers, MEV proposer control, configurable block times) the weighting can be skewed without sustaining the manipulated price, so a brief excursion gains outsized weight. This is distinct from the classic flash-loan spot-oracle hint because the violated trust is the temporal honesty of the sampling, not the instantaneous price; it is a time/state primitive failure that becomes exploitable precisely on the new high-throughput, single-sequencer venues protocols are migrating to. No oracle library validates inter-observation timing integrity against the sequencer, and no monitor flags a liquidation whose TWAP window was dominated by anomalously-spaced blocks.
suggested_research: Build a TWAP-window auditor that reconstructs the block-timestamp distribution feeding each oracle read and scores how concentrated the time-weight was on suspicious intervals, alerting on liquidations/mints whose window was cadence-skewed; on a single-sequencer L2 testbed, demonstrate value extraction by spacing blocks to over-weight a transient manipulated price without holding it.

trust_assumption: A cross-chain bridge that verifies source-chain events with an on-target light client trusts that a header/proof which passed the source chain's stated finality rule is irreversible, so once the light client accepts a deposit proof at the bridge's configured confirmation depth it irrevocably mints or releases the wrapped asset on the destination chain.
violation: An attacker exploits the gap between the bridge's hard-coded confirmation depth and the source chain's *actual* probabilistic or reconfigurable finality — triggering a deep reorg, a finality-gadget stall, or a validator-set rotation that orphans the proven block after the destination already minted — so a deposit that gets rolled back on the source chain has already been released on the destination, double-spending across the seam that assumes finality is a single global truth.

techniques.name: Source-chain deep reorg after destination mint at fixed confirmation depth
Light-client validator-set rotation orphaning a proven header

rationale: Light-client bridges are sold in 2025-2027 as the trust-minimized successor to multisig bridges, verifying real consensus proofs instead of trusting a federation. The unexamined seam is that 'finality' is not a uniform property: many chains offer only probabilistic finality, some have finality gadgets that can stall or revert under attack, and validator-set rotations can invalidate a header the light client already accepted. Bridges encode a static confirmation depth as a proxy for irreversibility, but that proxy can be wrong exactly when an attacker forces a reorg deeper than the configured threshold, and the destination mint is irreversible while the source deposit is not. This is distinct from the validator-quorum-forgery bridge hint because the cryptographic proof is honest and the quorum is not forged — the violated trust is the equivalence between 'passed my confirmation depth' and 'final on the source chain.' No bridge tooling adapts confirmation depth to live reorg risk, and no detector reconciles a destination mint against a subsequent source-chain reorg that orphaned its proof.
suggested_research: Build a cross-chain finality reconciler that tracks every bridge mint and watches the source chain for reorgs/finality reversions that orphan the proving block, raising a clawback alarm; model the economic conditions under which renting hashpower or stalling a finality gadget makes a reorg-after-mint profitable against named light-client bridges, and prototype dynamic confirmation depth keyed to live reorg depth.

trust_assumption: Users and integrators of an upgradeable proxy contract trust that the address they audited keeps its meaning across upgrades because the proxy's storage layout is append-only and the implementation is reviewed, so they treat the contract's persistent state (balances, roles, allowances) as stable and continue interacting with the same address after an upgrade without re-auditing how the new logic interprets existing storage slots.
violation: A proxy admin (or an attacker who captured the admin/upgrade key) ships a new implementation whose storage layout silently re-maps existing slots — reusing a slot that previously held a balance as a role flag, or shifting a struct so an attacker-controlled value now occupies the owner slot — so the same on-chain bytes that meant one thing under the audited logic are reinterpreted as authority or value under the new logic, with no bytecode-immutability violation visible at the trusted address.

techniques.name: Proxy implementation layout shift colliding persistent state

rationale: Upgradeable proxies (transparent, UUPS, beacon, diamond) are ubiquitous in 2025-2027 DeFi and the standard auditing mental model is 'audit the implementation, trust the admin not to rug.' The under-covered seam is not the well-known admin-key-swaps-logic risk but the format/boundary failure at the storage layer: persistent storage outlives the code that defined its layout, and an upgrade can reinterpret the *same bytes* as a different type or field — turning a stale balance slot into a role bitmap or pushing attacker data into the owner slot — without ever appearing as a bytecode change at the address users trust. This is a P5 reinterpretation distinct from the generic proxy-admin-key hint (which is about swapping logic wholesale) because here the attack is the silent semantic re-slotting of existing state that even a fresh audit of the new implementation may miss if it is not diffed against the live storage layout. Layout-diff tooling exists for deploys but is rarely enforced on live state, and no monitor alerts users that an upgrade changed the meaning of slots they hold value in.
suggested_research: Build a live storage-layout differ that, on every observed proxy upgrade, diffs the new implementation's slot map against the prior one over the contract's actual occupied storage and flags any slot whose type/semantics changed under live value; demonstrate a benign-looking upgrade that re-slots a balance mapping into a role flag to seize control without altering the trusted address.

trust_assumption: A contract that accepts an EIP-712 typed signature (permit, meta-transaction, gasless order, intent) trusts that the domain separator binding the signature to one chainId and one verifying contract guarantees the user's authorization can only be exercised in the single context the user signed for, so it executes the signed action as proof of scoped intent.
violation: An attacker replays a validly-signed EIP-712 message in a context the domain separator failed to actually pin to — a fork or rollup that shares the source chain's historical chainId, a redeployed verifying contract at the same address on a second chain via CREATE2, or a wallet that cached a stale domain across a chainId change — so a signature the human scoped to one chain/contract authorizes value movement on another, because the domain binding the verifier relies on collides across the multichain namespace the user never reasoned about.

techniques.name: EIP-712 signature replay across shared/forked chainId domain

rationale: EIP-712 typed signing is the backbone of 2025-2027 gasless UX, intents, and account abstraction, and the domain separator (name, version, chainId, verifyingContract) is treated as a complete replay guard. The seam is that the domain's uniqueness assumptions break in the multichain reality the standard predates: forks reuse historical chainIds, deterministic CREATE2 deploys put the same verifying contract address on many chains, and wallets cache domain data across network switches — so a signature scoped to a single context can be exercised in a colliding one. This is broader than the signature-replay hint's single-direction framing because it identifies the structural source of the collision (the multichain namespace versus a separator computed from values that are not globally unique) and includes the wallet-side stale-domain vector. It is a P2 identity/authority seam: the verifier trusts the domain as proof of scoped human intent. There is no tooling that audits a verifying contract's domain for cross-chain/fork collision exposure, and no detection correlates the same signed digest being consumed on two chains.
suggested_research: Build a domain-collision scanner that, for a given verifying contract, enumerates chains/forks where its chainId+address+name+version separator collides and flags exploitable replay surface; demonstrate cross-chain consumption of a single permit signature against a CREATE2-redeployed verifier, and propose a wallet-side domain-freshness check that re-derives chainId at sign time.

trust_assumption: A wallet user relies on the transaction-simulation / clear-signing preview the wallet renders (asset changes, recipients, decoded calldata) as a faithful prediction of what executing the transaction will do, so they approve based on the human-readable summary rather than the opaque calldata, treating 'simulation shows I receive X and send Y' as binding ground truth for on-chain effect.
violation: An attacker crafts calldata whose simulated execution diverges from its real execution — exploiting state that changes between simulation and inclusion (oracle/price reads, block.timestamp/number, a front-run state mutation, or a contract that branches on tx.origin/gasleft or a flash-set storage flag the simulator doesn't reproduce) — so the wallet's preview shows a benign, profitable-looking outcome while the mined transaction drains the signer, weaponizing the very simulation layer that was built to end blind signing.

techniques.name: Simulation/execution state divergence yielding decoy preview
Calldata branching on context absent in wallet simulation

rationale: Clear-signing and transaction simulation are the 2025-2027 industry answer to the blind-signing problem, and wallets/security extensions now present a decoded preview that users are explicitly told to trust over raw calldata. The under-explored seam is that a simulation is an evaluation against a snapshot of state, and on-chain execution happens later against different state under attacker influence — so a contract can be written to behave one way under the wallet's simulation conditions and another way at inclusion (reading a price the attacker will move, branching on a storage flag set by a sandwiching tx, or on tx ordering). The violated trust is data->control: the human treats the preview data as controlling their decision, while the attacker controls the gap between previewed and realized state. This is a distinct, newly-relevant seam created by the clear-signing rollout itself (the blind-signing hint assumed no preview at all); no wallet flags simulation/execution divergence risk, and no detection compares a signed tx's simulated preview to its mined effect.
suggested_research: Build a wallet-side divergence guard that re-simulates at inclusion-adjacent state (or against adversarial state perturbations) and warns when the realized outcome can deviate from the shown preview; demonstrate a contract whose simulation shows a gain but whose mined execution drains the signer by reading attacker-moved state, and quantify how many production wallets surface no divergence warning.

trust_assumption: A verifier of a W3C verifiable credential trusts that checking the issuer's signature plus the referenced status mechanism (StatusList2021 bitstring, on-chain revocation registry, did:method resolution) tells it the credential is currently valid, so a credential that resolves and whose status bit reads 'active' is accepted as a present-tense attestation of the holder's standing.
violation: An attacker exploits the propagation and resolution latency in the decentralized status layer — presenting a credential after revocation but before the status list/registry the verifier caches has updated, or steering the verifier to a stale did:method resolution or an outdated status-list mirror — so a revoked or rotated credential is honored as active, because in a decentralized identity system there is no synchronous authority that can answer 'is this valid right now' and the verifier silently trusts a snapshot that lags the true state.

techniques.name: Post-revocation credential acceptance during status-list propagation lag
Stale did/status-list resolution steered to honor revoked VC

rationale: Decentralized identity (DIDs and W3C verifiable credentials) is moving from pilots to real deployment in 2025-2027 (EU digital identity wallets, reusable KYC, employment/age credentials), and the verification model deliberately removes the central authority that PKI/OCSP-style systems queried for live status. The seam is that revocation in this world is eventually-consistent: status lists are published artifacts that verifiers fetch and cache, did:method resolution can be cached or steered, and there is no synchronous 'check now' — so the window between revocation and propagation is a designed-in feature, not a bug, that an attacker times against. The violated trust is the provenance/freshness of the status answer the verifier relies on. This is distinct from classic cert-revocation-latency because the status authority is itself decentralized and the verifier often cannot even reach a canonical current source. No DID wallet/verifier tooling measures or bounds status freshness, and no detection flags credentials presented during their revocation-propagation window.
suggested_research: Measure real-world status-propagation latency across StatusList2021 and on-chain revocation registries in shipping VC stacks, and build a verifier-side freshness bound that rejects or down-trusts credentials whose status snapshot is older than a policy threshold; demonstrate acceptance of a just-revoked credential by presenting it inside the propagation window against a caching verifier.

trust_assumption: A DAO's on-chain governance trusts that a token (or vote-escrowed/delegated) balance measured at a proposal's snapshot block represents a participant's genuine, at-risk economic stake, so it equates voting weight at that block with legitimate skin-in-the-game and lets that measured weight decide treasury, parameter, and upgrade outcomes.
violation: An attacker assembles voting weight they have no lasting stake in — flash-borrowing governance tokens, renting delegated voting power, or momentarily acquiring vote-escrow positions just before the snapshot block and unwinding immediately after — so the snapshot reads attacker-controlled weight as legitimate stake and passes a self-dealing proposal, because the governance system trusts a point-in-time balance as a proxy for durable economic alignment it never verifies the persistence of.

techniques.name: Rented delegated voting power transient to the snapshot block

rationale: On-chain DAO governance controls enormous treasuries and protocol-upgrade keys in 2025-2027, and the security model assumes voting weight equals economic stake equals aligned incentives. The seam is that balance at a snapshot block is a fungible, rentable, flash-loanable quantity — markets now exist to borrow governance tokens and rent delegated/vote-escrow power for a single block — so an attacker can present overwhelming 'stake' at the measurement instant with zero durable exposure, then unwind. The violated trust is the identity/authority equivalence between a transient measured balance and persistent skin-in-the-game. While the governance-flash-borrow hint names the primitive, the under-tooled extension here is that even time-locked or vote-escrow and delegation designs are gameable via rental markets and just-in-time escrow, and there is no tooling that scores a proposal's passing coalition for stake transience. No governance framework verifies durable holding across the proposal lifecycle, and no monitor flags votes whose weight was borrowed/rented around the snapshot.
suggested_research: Build a governance-stake-persistence analyzer that, for each passed proposal, traces whether the deciding voters held their weight durably or acquired/borrowed it around the snapshot and unwound after, scoring coalition transience; prototype a time-integrated voting weight (stake held across a window, not a single block) and demonstrate capture of a vanilla snapshot vote via flash-rented delegation.

trust_assumption: An ERC-4337 paymaster that agrees to sponsor a user operation's gas trusts that the conditions it checked in the validation phase — the userOp's calldata, the account's state, an off-chain signed sponsorship policy, or a token allowance it will pull as repayment — still hold when the operation executes, so it commits to paying gas based on the picture it validated.
violation: An attacker exploits the structural separation between ERC-4337's validation and execution phases (and the bundler's freedom to order/aggregate userOps) so that state the paymaster relied on during validation is changed before or during execution — the repayment token is drained, the sponsored action is swapped via reentrant or ordering effects, or aggregated userOps interfere — leaving the paymaster to pay gas for an operation that no longer matches what it agreed to sponsor, draining its deposit at scale.

techniques.name: Validation/execution state divergence draining paymaster deposit

rationale: Account abstraction via ERC-4337 is hitting production wallets and chains in 2025-2027, and paymasters that sponsor gas are central to the gasless UX everyone is building. The protocol deliberately splits a userOp into a validation phase (where the paymaster decides to pay) and an execution phase (where the action runs), with strict storage-access rules during validation that prevent the paymaster from fully foreseeing execution-time state — and bundlers can order and aggregate userOps between those phases. The seam is context-inheritance: the paymaster's commitment to pay inherits the validation-time context, but execution runs in a context the attacker can mutate (drain the repayment token, change what the sponsored call does, exploit ordering), so the paymaster pays for something other than what it vetted. This is a fresh AA-specific seam with essentially no tooling; the paymaster-trust hint names the actor but not this validation/execution-context divergence. No paymaster framework re-checks preconditions at execution, and no detection flags sponsored ops whose execution-time state contradicts validation-time assumptions.
suggested_research: Enumerate paymaster designs and identify which sponsorship preconditions can be invalidated between validation and execution under hostile bundler ordering and reentrancy, then build a paymaster-side guard that re-asserts critical invariants at execution start and a detector for sponsored ops whose repayment or sponsored action diverged; demonstrate deposit drain by mutating the repayment-token balance after validation.

trust_assumption: A DePIN network's on-chain reward contract trusts that the proofs of physical work it receives (sensor readings, location/GPS attestations, coverage or bandwidth measurements, hashed device telemetry) are honest evidence that a real device performed real-world work at a real place and time, so it mints token rewards in proportion to those submitted proofs.
violation: An attacker fabricates physical-work proofs without doing the work — spoofing GNSS/location, replaying or simulating sensor telemetry, virtualizing many device identities on one host, or colluding so witnesses attest to non-existent coverage — and the reward contract mints tokens because on-chain it cannot distinguish a cryptographically well-formed proof of real physical work from a well-formed fabrication, since the gap between the digital attestation and the physical ground truth is unverifiable from the chain's vantage point.

techniques.name: GNSS/location-spoofed proof of physical work minting rewards
Virtualized multi-identity device sybil for coverage rewards

rationale: DePIN (decentralized physical infrastructure: wireless coverage, mapping, sensing, compute, energy) is a fast-growing 2025-2027 sector that pays token rewards for real-world work attested on-chain. The fundamental seam is provenance across the digital/physical boundary: a smart contract can verify a signature and a proof's structure but cannot itself perceive whether a sensor reading, a GPS fix, or a coverage measurement reflects reality, so a cryptographically valid proof of fabricated work is indistinguishable from honest work at the point of minting. Attackers spoof location, replay/simulate telemetry, virtualize device fleets, or collude on witness attestations to farm rewards. This generalizes and extends the DePIN-sensor-reward hint by framing it as a P3 ground-truth gap inherent to any chain that mints on physical attestations, and by spanning location, telemetry, and sybil/virtualization vectors. There is little tooling to detect fabricated physical-work proofs at scale, detection is only nascent (some networks do anomaly heuristics), and no general framework binds an on-chain proof to verifiable physical ground truth.
suggested_research: Build a cross-network physical-work fraud detector that fuses spatial-temporal plausibility (impossible travel, implausible coverage density), device-fingerprint sybil clustering, and signal-physics consistency to flag fabricated proofs, and evaluate it against named DePIN reward systems; demonstrate reward farming via GNSS spoofing plus virtualized device identities, and propose hardware-rooted attestation that binds proofs to tamper-evident physical sensing.
